intro:
Noah (Hebrew Noach, "rest" or "comfort") was the tenth patriarch from Adam, the son of Lamech, and the man through whom God preserved the human race and the animal world through the Flood. His father named him in hope that "this one shall bring us relief from our work and from the painful toil of our hands" (Genesis 5:29). In an age of total corruption, Noah stands as the great Old Testament example of righteousness, faith, and obedience — a second head of humanity and a recipient of covenant grace.

The Genesis narrative sets Noah against a world that had grown so violent that "every intention of the thoughts of [man's] heart was only evil continually" (Genesis 6:5). Against that darkness, "Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD" (Genesis 6:8) — the first appearance of grace in the Bible — and is described as "a righteous man, blameless in his generation," who "walked with God" (Genesis 6:9). Warned of the coming judgment, he obeyed God's detailed command to build an ark of gopher wood, and "Noah did all that God commanded him" (Genesis 6:22). Through the ark he, his wife, his three sons — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — and their wives, together with pairs of every kind of creature, were carried safely through the waters that destroyed the corrupt world.

When the flood subsided and the ark rested on the mountains of Ararat, Noah sent out the raven and the dove, and at last went out onto the cleansed earth, where he built an altar and offered sacrifice (Genesis 8:20). God responded with a great covenant — the first formally named covenant in Scripture — promising never again to destroy all life by flood, renewing the creation mandate to "be fruitful and multiply," and giving the rainbow as its enduring sign (Genesis 9:11–13). Yet Noah was not sinless: he planted a vineyard, became drunk, and the shameful episode that followed brought a curse on Canaan and shaped the destinies of his sons' descendants (Genesis 9:20–27). He lived 950 years (Genesis 9:29).

Noah's significance reaches across both Testaments. Ezekiel ranks him with Daniel and Job as a byword for righteousness (Ezekiel 14:14). Hebrews praises him as a man of faith who, "being warned by God concerning events as yet unseen," built the ark and "became an heir of the righteousness that comes by faith" (Hebrews 11:7); Peter calls him "a herald of righteousness" (2 Peter 2:5). Jesus made the complacency of "the days of Noah" a warning picture of the world at the coming of the Son of Man (Matthew 24:37–39), and Peter saw in the waters that bore up the ark a foreshadowing of baptism and salvation through judgment (1 Peter 3:20–21). Thus Noah stands as both a historical deliverer and a type of the salvation God works for those who walk with Him by faith.
